baseline: Walks slowly but steadily with a walker during daytime routines.
Usually accepts evening medication when a familiar nurse explains the sequence first.
Often rests after lunch and prefers unhurried transitions.
communication_cues: Use short sentences and pause before repeating a question.
Approach from the front, introduce yourself, and explain the next care step before touching equipment.
Offer either-or choices instead of open-ended prompts during medication and bathing routines.
preferences: Prefers the room door partly closed during evening care.
Responds well to warm tea after meals.
Likes hearing that her daughter Mika was updated when family context is relevant.
known_triggers: Corridor noise near the room can increase agitation.
Unfamiliar staff moving quickly during medication rounds can lead to refusal.
Unexpected walker repositioning can make transfers feel unsafe.
calming_approaches: Lower room noise, sit at eye level, and restart the explanation slowly.
Give a short break before re-offering medication with a familiar staff member when possible.
Confirm where the walker is before asking her to stand.
family_context_notes: Daughter Mika is the primary family contact and usually visits on Sunday afternoons.
Family reports that quiet music after dinner has helped with settling in the past.
recent_history: Recent notes show slower gait after lunch and near hallway turns.
Two recent evening notes include the same medication refusal phrase.
Noise from the corridor was linked to a request for a quieter room.
watch_patterns: Track whether gait slowness appears after lunch or also during morning care.
Watch for repeated medication refusal when unfamiliar staff or corridor noise are present.
Escalate only as an operational handoff concern; avoid diagnostic or prescribing language.
